C2.instruction: Click any word you think is a fake English word. It must look like real English but not exist in any dictionary. There are exactly 10.
C2.passage: The relentless proliferation of digital media has precipitated a profound shift in our collective psychology, ostensibly designed to foster unprecedented global connectivity. However, beneath this veneer of limitless interaction lies a pervasive ephemerance, where substantive discourse is rapidly supplanted by fleeting, superficial engagements. We are frequently confronted by the ostentatiary displays of curated personas, which only serve to exacerbate the latent cognitive dissonance experienced by the average individual. Despite concerted efforts to achieve some ameliorance of this digital fatigue, the sheer vociferity of the online ecosystem drowns out nuanced contemplation. In our pursuit of serendipital discoveries within the algorithmic expanse, we often find ourselves entangled in an ineffablistic web of data that defies logical categorization. This relentless bombardment demands a high degree of cognitancy just to navigate the daily barrage of information, yet the inherent obfuscity of platform algorithms ensures that true clarity remains elusive. Consequently, what initially sparked a sense of global ebulliment has, for many, devolved into a stubborn intransigism, leaving society fundamentally fragmented despite its illusion of absolute cohesion.

A1.instruction: What does Tom do every morning before breakfast?
options: He takes Max for a walk
A1.correct: He takes Max for a walk
A1.text: Tom has a dog called Max. Max is brown and very friendly. Every morning, before breakfast, Tom takes Max for a walk in the park. Max loves the park because there are many other dogs to play with. Tom always brings a ball for Max to catch.

Tom has a dog called Max. Max is brown and very friendly. Every morning, before breakfast, Tom takes Max for a walk in the park. Max loves the park because there are many other dogs to play with. Tom always brings a ball for Max to catch.

A2.instruction: Why did Maria move to London?
A2.text: Maria moved to London three years ago to look for work. At first, she found the city very big and lonely — she missed her family and the food from home. However, after a few months, she made some good friends at her new job. Now she loves the city and cannot imagine living anywhere else.

A2.instruction: How did Maria's feelings about London change over time?
options: She went from feeling lonely to loving it
A2.correct: She went from feeling lonely to loving it

B1.instruction: What do critics say about recycling campaigns?
options: They make people believe the problem is under control when it is not
They are too expensive to run
They focus too much on corporations
B1.correct: They make people believe the problem is under control when it is not
B1.text: Many governments have launched campaigns encouraging citizens to recycle. However, critics argue these campaigns give people a false sense that the plastic problem is being managed, when in reality less than 10% of all plastic ever produced has been recycled. Recycling alone, they say, cannot solve the crisis — a fundamental rethinking of production and consumption is required. Asking people to simply "sort their bins" may let corporations off the hook entirely.

B1.instruction: What does the phrase "sort their bins" suggest about the author's view?
options: Sorting bins is the most important action
Recycling alone is not enough to solve the crisis
Governments should do more bin collection
People are too lazy to recycle
B1.correct: Recycling alone is not enough to solve the crisis
B1.text: Many governments have launched campaigns encouraging citizens to recycle. However, critics argue these campaigns give people a false sense that the plastic problem is being managed, when in reality less than 10% of all plastic ever produced has been recycled. Recycling alone, they say, cannot solve the crisis — a fundamental rethinking of production and consumption is required. Asking people to simply "sort their bins" may let corporations off the hook entirely.

B1.instruction: What percentage of plastic has ever been recycled?

However, critics argue these campaigns give people a false sense that the plastic problem is being managed, when in reality less than 10% of all plastic ever produced has been recycled. Recycling alone, they say, cannot solve the crisis — a fundamental rethinking of production and consumption is required. Asking people to simply "sort their bins" may let corporations off the hook entirely.

B2.instruction: What is "the paradox" referred to in the passage?
options: Social media is popular but boring
Platforms designed to connect people are making them more isolated
People use social media but don't like it
B2.correct: Platforms designed to connect people are making them more isolated
B2.text: Research consistently links heavy social media use to rising rates of anxiety, depression, and loneliness — yet these same platforms were designed to connect people. This is the paradox at the heart of the debate. Some researchers point to amplification effects, arguing that platforms worsen pre-existing mental health conditions rather than cause new ones. Others focus on addictive design patterns: infinite scroll, variable reward notifications, and algorithmically curated content that maximises time on screen. Governments have begun calling for independent audits, though whether these will lead to binding regulation remains deeply uncertain.

B2.instruction: What does the final sentence imply?
options: Regulation is certain to come soon
The harm is proven and action will definitely be taken
There is doubt that audits will lead to real legal change
Governments are not interested in the issue
B2.correct: There is doubt that audits will lead to real legal change
B2.text: Research consistently links heavy social media use to rising rates of anxiety, depression, and loneliness — yet these same platforms were designed to connect people. This is the paradox at the heart of the debate. Some researchers point to amplification effects, arguing that platforms worsen pre-existing mental health conditions rather than cause new ones. Others focus on addictive design patterns: infinite scroll, variable reward notifications, and algorithmically curated content that maximises time on screen. Governments have begun calling for independent audits, though whether these will lead to binding regulation remains deeply uncertain.

B2.instruction: Which two explanations for the link between social media and mental health are mentioned?
options: Amplification of pre-existing conditions and addictive design patterns
Poor content quality and too much advertising
B2.correct: Amplification of pre-existing conditions and addictive design patterns
B2.text: Research consistently links heavy social media use to rising rates of anxiety, depression, and loneliness — yet these same platforms were designed to connect people. This is the paradox at the heart of the debate. Some researchers point to amplification effects, arguing that platforms worsen pre-existing mental health conditions rather than cause new ones. Others focus on addictive design patterns: infinite scroll, variable reward notifications, and algorithmically curated content that maximises time on screen. Governments have begun calling for independent audits, though whether these will lead to binding regulation remains deeply uncertain.

C1.instruction: What assumption in the pro-UBI argument do critics question?
options: That automation is bad for workers
That previous disruptions were less severe than today's
That automation is unprecedented, when historically economies adapt
C1.correct: That automation is unprecedented, when historically economies adapt
C1.text: Proponents of Universal Basic Income argue that the rise of automation represents an unprecedented threat to employment — one that traditional welfare systems are not equipped to handle. Critics, however, question the underlying assumption: that this wave of technological disruption is fundamentally different from previous ones. The Industrial Revolution displaced the weavers of the 1800s, yet new industries emerged. The certainty of catastrophe, these critics suggest, is no more defensible than complacency. What is clear is that the debate turns not on whether jobs will change — they will — but on whether societies will act with sufficient foresight to manage that change humanely.

C1.instruction: What does "the certainty of catastrophe is no more defensible than complacency" suggest about the author?
options: The author thinks catastrophe is certain
The author is balanced and avoids taking a firm side
The author believes we should do nothing
C1.correct: The author is balanced and avoids taking a firm side
C1.text: Proponents of Universal Basic Income argue that the rise of automation represents an unprecedented threat to employment — one that traditional welfare systems are not equipped to handle. Critics, however, question the underlying assumption: that this wave of technological disruption is fundamentally different from previous ones. The Industrial Revolution displaced the weavers of the 1800s, yet new industries emerged. The certainty of catastrophe, these critics suggest, is no more defensible than complacency. What is clear is that the debate turns not on whether jobs will change — they will — but on whether societies will act with sufficient foresight to manage that change humanely.

C1.instruction: What historical example is used and what point does it make?
options: The French Revolution — showing that change is violent
Industrial Revolution weavers — showing that past displacement did not prevent new industries from emerging
The invention of the internet — showing technology always creates jobs
World War II — showing that economies can recover quickly
C1.correct: Industrial Revolution weavers — showing that past displacement did not prevent new industries from emerging
C1.text: Proponents of Universal Basic Income argue that the rise of automation represents an unprecedented threat to employment — one that traditional welfare systems are not equipped to handle. Critics, however, question the underlying assumption: that this wave of technological disruption is fundamentally different from previous ones. The Industrial Revolution displaced the weavers of the 1800s, yet new industries emerged. The certainty of catastrophe, these critics suggest, is no more defensible than complacency. What is clear is that the debate turns not on whether jobs will change — they will — but on whether societies will act with sufficient foresight to manage that change humanely.

C2.instruction: What is "counter-majoritarianism" as used in the passage?
options: A theory that courts should have more power than parliaments
The accusation that courts override elected majorities with their own preferences
The view that minorities should be protected from majority rule
A political movement opposed to constitutional reform
C2.correct: The accusation that courts override elected majorities with their own preferences
C2.text: The tension between judicial independence and democratic accountability has never been fully resolved in liberal constitutional theory. Courts that strike down legislation passed by elected majorities inevitably attract the charge of counter-majoritarianism — the accusation that an unelected judiciary is substituting its own preferences for those of the people. Yet defenders of judicial review contend that democracy is not reducible to majority rule: if constitutional rights are to mean anything, they must be enforceable even against the wishes of a transient parliamentary majority. The debate thus forces us to confront a prior question: what do we mean, precisely, when we invoke "democracy"?

C2.instruction: How do defenders of judicial review respond to the charge?
options: They argue courts always reflect public opinion
They deny that courts ever strike down legislation
They argue democracy means more than majority rule and rights must be protected from transient majorities
They argue the judiciary should be elected
C2.correct: They argue democracy means more than majority rule and rights must be protected from transient majorities
C2.text: The tension between judicial independence and democratic accountability has never been fully resolved in liberal constitutional theory. Courts that strike down legislation passed by elected majorities inevitably attract the charge of counter-majoritarianism — the accusation that an unelected judiciary is substituting its own preferences for those of the people. Yet defenders of judicial review contend that democracy is not reducible to majority rule: if constitutional rights are to mean anything, they must be enforceable even against the wishes of a transient parliamentary majority. The debate thus forces us to confront a prior question: what do we mean, precisely, when we invoke "democracy"?

C2.instruction: What "prior question" does the author say the debate forces us to confront?
options: What we actually mean by the concept of democracy itself
Whether rights should be written into law
Whether judges should be politically neutral
C2.correct: What we actually mean by the concept of democracy itself
C2.text: The tension between judicial independence and democratic accountability has never been fully resolved in liberal constitutional theory. Courts that strike down legislation passed by elected majorities inevitably attract the charge of counter-majoritarianism — the accusation that an unelected judiciary is substituting its own preferences for those of the people. Yet defenders of judicial review contend that democracy is not reducible to majority rule: if constitutional rights are to mean anything, they must be enforceable even against the wishes of a transient parliamentary majority. The debate thus forces us to confront a prior question: what do we mean, precisely, when we invoke "democracy"?

options: Second floor, next to the stairs

A1.text:
Lucy: Hi James! You look a bit lost — is everything okay?

Lucy: No problem! It's on the second floor, next to the stairs.

Lucy: Don't worry, you'll get used to it quickly!

A2.instruction: What is the problem with the jacket?
options: There is a hole in it
A2.text:
Customer: Excuse me. I bought this jacket here last week, but there's a problem with it.
Assistant: Oh, I'm sorry to hear that. What seems to be the issue?
Customer: The zip is broken. It got stuck the first time I used it.
Assistant: That's not acceptable at all. Would you like a refund or an exchange?
Customer: I really like the jacket, so I'd prefer to exchange it if possible.
Assistant: Of course. Let me check if we have your size in stock.

B1.instruction: Why has the employee been arriving late?
B1.text:
Manager: I wanted to have a quick word about the recent lateness. You've been late three times this week.
Employee: I know, and I'm really sorry. There are roadworks near my house and the bus has been severely delayed every morning.
Manager: I see. I appreciate you explaining. Have you looked at other routes?
Employee: I've tried, but they all add at least 40 minutes.
Manager: In that case, what if you worked from home on Mondays and Fridays for the next month? We can review after that.
Employee: That would be incredibly helpful. Thank you for being so understanding.

B1.instruction: What does the manager's response reveal about their approach?
options: They want to fire the employee
They don't care about the issue

B2.instruction: Name two things the candidate did during their gap year.
options: Completed an analytics course and volunteered at a charity
Took care of family and freelanced
B2.correct: Completed an analytics course and volunteered at a charity
B2.text:
Interviewer: During your CV gap, what were you actually doing?
Candidate: I completed a professional analytics course online and spent six months volunteering with a local charity, independently managing their donor communications.
Interviewer: Interesting. When you say "independently" — do you mean you had formal oversight, or were you entirely self-directed?
Candidate: Entirely self-directed. I set my own targets, reported to no one, and built their system from scratch.
Interviewer: And how does that period connect to the role you're applying for today?
Candidate: It was entirely intentional. I knew I wanted to move into data-driven communications, and I used the gap to build the skills I was missing.

B2.instruction: Why does the interviewer ask about the word "independently"?
options: They think the candidate is lying
They want to check whether it means formally supervised or self-directed
They don't know what the word means
They are testing the candidate's language skills
B2.correct: They want to check whether it means formally supervised or self-directed

Candidate: It was entirely intentional. I knew I wanted to move into data-driven communications, and I used the gap to build the skills I was missing.

It proves they were unemployable elsewhere
It shows they prefer working alone
B2.correct: It shows the gap was intentional and designed to build relevant skills

C1.instruction: What technique does the journalist use in their opening question?
options: They ask a vague general question
They use specific evidence (four consecutive years) to challenge the minister's credibility
They express sympathy for the minister's situation
They ask the minister to explain government policy
C1.correct: They use specific evidence (four consecutive years) to challenge the minister's credibility
C1.text:
Journalist: Minister, the housing targets were missed for the fourth consecutive year. At what point does a target become meaningless?
Minister: I understand the frustration, but these are complex structural issues. We remain fully committed to the programme.
Journalist: You told Parliament last spring you were "confident" the targets would be met. Were you confident?
Minister: I was confident in the direction of the programme, yes.
Journalist: Confident, or certain?
Minister: Well... I was confident we had the right approach. Whether every figure would be hit — I couldn't be certain of that.

C1.instruction: What does the minister's final answer reveal?
options: They did not have guaranteed certainty — "confident" was overstated
They blame the journalist for misquoting them
C1.correct: They did not have guaranteed certainty — "confident" was overstated
C1.text:
Journalist: Minister, the housing targets were missed for the fourth consecutive year. At what point does a target become meaningless?
Minister: I understand the frustration, but these are complex structural issues. We remain fully committed to the programme.
Journalist: You told Parliament last spring you were "confident" the targets would be met. Were you confident?
Minister: I was confident in the direction of the programme, yes.
Journalist: Confident, or certain?
Minister: Well... I was confident we had the right approach. Whether every figure would be hit — I couldn't be certain of that.

C1.instruction: What is the purpose of pressing the minister on "confident" vs "certain"?
options: To show that the journalist is pedantic
To expose the gap between public confidence and private doubt
C1.correct: To expose the gap between public confidence and private doubt
C1.text:
Journalist: Minister, the housing targets were missed for the fourth consecutive year. At what point does a target become meaningless?
Minister: I understand the frustration, but these are complex structural issues. We remain fully committed to the programme.
Journalist: You told Parliament last spring you were "confident" the targets would be met. Were you confident?
Minister: I was confident in the direction of the programme, yes.
Journalist: Confident, or certain?
Minister: Well... I was confident we had the right approach. Whether every figure would be hit — I couldn't be certain of that.

C2.instruction: What specific methodological conflict does the Chair identify?
options: The professor used the wrong statistics
The Cochrane review contradicts the professor's "robust" claim, given it rated evidence as low-certainty
The professor has no peer-reviewed publications
The professor used data from a different country
C2.correct: The Cochrane review contradicts the professor's "robust" claim, given it rated evidence as low-certainty
C2.text:
Chair: Professor, your paper claims the evidence for this intervention is "robust." The Cochrane review published last March rated it as low-certainty. How do you reconcile those characterisations?
Professor: The Cochrane review used a GRADE framework that penalises heterogeneity across trials. Our meta-analysis controlled for baseline confounds.
Chair: Controlled for, or adjusted post-hoc?
Professor: The adjustments were pre-registered in our protocol.
Chair: Registered before or after you saw the data?
Professor: Before final analysis, yes — though some preliminary figures were available at the time of registration.
Chair: That's not pre-registration as it is ordinarily understood in the field, is it?

C2.instruction: What does the Chair's final question imply about the professor's pre-registration?
options: The professor correctly followed the protocol
The professor's pre-registration is technically accurate but does not meet the standard the field actually requires
The professor registered the wrong protocol
C2.correct: The professor's pre-registration is technically accurate but does not meet the standard the field actually requires
C2.text:
Chair: Professor, your paper claims the evidence for this intervention is "robust." The Cochrane review published last March rated it as low-certainty. How do you reconcile those characterisations?
Professor: The Cochrane review used a GRADE framework that penalises heterogeneity across trials. Our meta-analysis controlled for baseline confounds.
Chair: Controlled for, or adjusted post-hoc?
Professor: The adjustments were pre-registered in our protocol.
Chair: Registered before or after you saw the data?
Professor: Before final analysis, yes — though some preliminary figures were available at the time of registration.
Chair: That's not pre-registration as it is ordinarily understood in the field, is it?

C2.instruction: How does the dialogue structure reveal the Chair's strategy?
options: The Chair attacks the professor personally
The Chair asks vague general questions
The Chair progressively narrows each answer to expose a logical inconsistency about methodology and research integrity
The Chair agrees with the professor at every step
C2.correct: The Chair progressively narrows each answer to expose a logical inconsistency about methodology and research integrity
C2.text:
Chair: Professor, your paper claims the evidence for this intervention is "robust." The Cochrane review published last March rated it as low-certainty. How do you reconcile those characterisations?
Professor: The Cochrane review used a GRADE framework that penalises heterogeneity across trials. Our meta-analysis controlled for baseline confounds.
Chair: Controlled for, or adjusted post-hoc?
Professor: The adjustments were pre-registered in our protocol.
Chair: Registered before or after you saw the data?
Professor: Before final analysis, yes — though some preliminary figures were available at the time of registration.
Chair: That's not pre-registration as it is ordinarily understood in the field, is it?

A1.instruction: Listen to the passage about a morning routine. Based on what you hear, how does the person truly feel about the bus being late?
options: They hate it because it makes them late for work
They don't mind it because it gives them time to rest
They enjoy it because they can eat breakfast on the bus
They are confused because the bus is never on time
A1.correct: They don't mind it because it gives them time to rest

A1.instruction: Listen again. What does the narrator do during the morning commute?
options: Eat a big breakfast and read the news
Drink coffee, run to the bus, and sleep on the way
Walk slowly while listening to music
Take a taxi to avoid being late
A1.correct: Drink coffee, run to the bus, and sleep on the way

A1.instruction: Listen to the passage about shopping. Why didn't the person buy any shoes?
options: They decided they didn't need new shoes after all
The store was closed when they arrived
The nice shoes were too expensive and the cheap ones were uncomfortable
A1.correct: The nice shoes were too expensive and the cheap ones were uncomfortable

A2.instruction: Listen to the passage about an evening at home. What is the real reason the person left the dishes in the sink?
options: They wanted to relax and watch their game because it was getting late
They broke the kitchen light and couldn't see to clean
They expected someone else to wash them
They realized they needed to buy soap the next morning
A2.correct: They wanted to relax and watch their game because it was getting late

A2.instruction: Listen again. What can be inferred about the narrator's priorities at that moment?
options: They felt guilty about not cleaning up
They valued their own comfort and leisure over the chore
They planned to wake up early and do the dishes
They were angry about having to cook dinner
A2.correct: They valued their own comfort and leisure over the chore

A2.instruction: Listen to the passage about a party. What can be inferred about Sarah's departure?
options: She suddenly became very ill and needed medical attention
She was not enjoying the loud crowded environment
Her brother had an emergency and forced her to leave
She was angry because the host played bad music
A2.correct: She was not enjoying the loud crowded environment

B1.instruction: Listen to the passage about a new office. What is the underlying problem the narrator is facing?
options: They are unhappy that the new office is too far from home
They dislike the taste of the new espresso machine
The open office design lacks the privacy and quiet needed to concentrate
They are trying to avoid a specific coworker
B1.correct: The open office design lacks the privacy and quiet needed to concentrate

B1.instruction: Listen again. Why did the narrator schedule a meeting with their manager?
options: To complain about the printer placement
To request permission to work remotely on certain days
To ask for a desk relocation away from the hallway
B1.correct: To request permission to work remotely on certain days

B1.instruction: Listen to the passage about a planned trip. Why did the narrator suggest taking the train instead of driving?
options: They wanted to enjoy the snowy scenery without focusing on the road
They recognized that driving their car in heavy snow would be dangerous
They thought the extra bags would be too heavy for the car
Their partner dislikes driving long distances in winter
B1.correct: They recognized that driving their car in heavy snow would be dangerous

B2.instruction: Listen to the passage about a corporate merger. What do the senior employees anticipate will happen?
options: They expect to be promoted to middle management
They anticipate that the merger will result in layoffs
They think the CEO is planning to leave the company
They believe the rival firm will cancel the merger
B2.correct: They anticipate that the merger will result in layoffs

B2.instruction: Listen again. Why are the senior employees speaking in hushed tones?
options: They don't want the CEO to hear their concerns
They are discussing a secret project
They are afraid of being overheard by rival firm spies
The breakroom has a quiet policy
B2.correct: They don't want the CEO to hear their concerns

B2.instruction: Based on the passage, what does the CEO's enthusiastic email suggest?
options: The company is honestly preparing employees for changes
The company is using positive language to hide potential negative outcomes
The company wants everyone to celebrate the merger
The company is excited about new business partnerships
B2.correct: The company is using positive language to hide potential negative outcomes

C1.instruction: Listen to the passage about a restaurant. What is the implicit message regarding the restaurant's business practices?
options: The restaurant is expanding to serve out-of-state customers
The owner is buying the regional farms to ensure supply
The restaurant relies on commercial suppliers despite its local branding
The restaurant's farm-to-table marketing is deceptive
C1.correct: The restaurant's farm-to-table marketing is deceptive

C1.instruction: Listen again. How does the owner's response to the food blogger reveal the truth?
options: It demonstrates a genuine commitment to quality ingredients
The vague justification suggests an attempt to deflect scrutiny
It shows the restaurant is transparent about its supply chain
The owner is proud of the seasonal menu variety
C1.correct: The vague justification suggests an attempt to deflect scrutiny

C1.instruction: Listen to the passage about a new library. What is the author's primary underlying criticism of the project?
options: It prioritized modern aesthetics over essential community support services
The glass and steel design is inferior to the historic brick building
The politicians failed to provide enough books for the library
The silent atmosphere is too intimidating for local children
C1.correct: It prioritized modern aesthetics over essential community support services

C2.instruction: Listen to the passage about educational reform. According to the passage, what was the actual result of the reform?
options: A truly equal educational system that closed the socioeconomic gap
Higher levels of critical thinking among students
The complete abandonment of standardized testing
A superficial statistical success that stifled genuine learning
C2.correct: A superficial statistical success that stifled genuine learning

C2.instruction: Listen again. What does the author imply about the phrase 'standardize excellence'?
options: It accurately describes the reform's successful outcomes
It is a misleading slogan used to promote a flawed policy
It represents a breakthrough in educational measurement
It helped teachers focus on what matters most
C2.correct: It is a misleading slogan used to promote a flawed policy

C2.instruction: Based on the passage, what was the 'profound unmeasured casualty' of the reform?
options: The loss of funding for arts programs
The decline in teacher morale and retention
The sacrifice of intellectual curiosity and deep learning
The increase in dropout rates among disadvantaged students
C2.correct: The sacrifice of intellectual curiosity and deep learning
